So, the task is story, concept, content, slogan, and key selling points. A whole package. And the gameplay style can be anything from action to life simulation. That's truly wide open. A horror-themed *life simulation*, for instance, could be fascinating. Or a mystery game where the true horror is the revelation of human nature.

When it comes to consulting, my first thought goes to someone who might also appreciate a deeper dive into ideas, rather than just quick answers. Kobayashi-kun seems like a good choice there. He's younger, but his reflective work style suggests he'd be open to discussing concepts in detail, maybe even challenging my thoughts in a constructive way. He might bring a fresh, modern perspective to horror while still appreciating the psychological depth I'm keen on exploring. We could bounce ideas off each other without feeling rushed, really getting into the "why" behind an idea. It wouldn't be about just pitching a final idea, but about exploring the territory together.

After I've had some initial back-and-forth with Kobayashi-kun, and once I've had more time to solidify some directions, I might then think about bringing in Sasaki-san or Yoshida-san. Sasaki-san, being the most senior, might offer invaluable wisdom on what's worked in the past, or how to refine a concept for broader appeal, considering the market. Yoshida-san, with his balanced approach, could help ground some of the more "out there" ideas, offering a practical perspective on feasibility or potential pitfalls.

As for Yamaguchi-san, our leader, I'll definitely want to have well-formed ideas to present to her. Her intuitive nature means she's probably great at quickly grasping the essence of a concept, but as a leader, she'll need solid foundations to summarize for the company. I'll aim to have a compelling narrative hook, a unique gameplay loop, and a clear vision of the experience we want players to have, ready for her to build upon.

At 63, I've seen a fair few games come and go, so I'm keen to see what fresh takes we can cook up.

First things first, what exactly am I designing? Story, concept, contents, slogan, key selling points. That's the whole package, really. Not just a mechanic, but the soul of the game. And the gameplay style is completely open. That's where the "outside the box" comes in, I suppose. Could be anything from an FPS to a life sim.

My mind's already buzzing with a few vague notions. Since we're in horror, what if we twisted that? Instead of being overtly scary, what if it's a horror game that *doesn't look like a horror game* at first glance? Something insidious, or maybe a life simulation where the horror slowly creeps in, almost imperceptibly? Or even a cooperative puzzle game where the real threat isn't monsters, but trust issues or a sense of impending doom that builds up without jump scares. I like the idea of subverting expectations. A horror game for people who *think* they don't like horror, perhaps. Or one that plays on psychological dread rather than gore.

Now, who to consult first? I've got my department colleagues here. There's Kato, Yoshida, Yamada, and our leader, Yamaguchi.
Yamaguchi is the leader, and she's quite young at 23, and intuitive. She'll be summarizing everything, so I'll definitely be talking to her eventually to get her overall vision and present my ideas.
Kato is 38 and intuitive. He's younger than me, full of energy I bet, and that "intuitive" workstyle suggests he'll be good at throwing out wild, creative ideas. Given the "free ideas, think outside the box" directive, I think he'd be an excellent starting point for just brainstorming some raw, unrefined concepts. Yoshida, he's 52, balanced, like me. He might be a good sounding board later on, someone to bounce more developed ideas off of, someone who can see things from both the creative and practical sides.
Yamada, 60, reflective. She'd be great for deep dives, for poking holes in concepts, for really thinking through the implications. I'd bring more developed ideas to her for refinement.

So, for kicking off this "free ideas" phase, I think I'll want to tap into some pure creative energy. I'm leaning towards chatting with **Kato first**. He's intuitive, younger, and probably has a lot of fresh ideas brewing without too many preconceptions. I want to cast a wide net initially, and his perspective would be valuable for that.

As for how I'll approach this task myself? I'm a balanced worker, so I like a bit of structure, but also appreciate flexibility. I'll spend some time letting my own mind wander, jotting down any random concepts that come to me – no matter how silly they seem. Then, I'll talk to Kato, maybe just a casual chat to see what kind of wild stuff he's been thinking about. I won't go in with fully formed proposals, but more with open questions like, "Hey, the boss wants us to really break the mold this time, what's the craziest idea for a game you've ever had, especially if it started as horror but then just went off the rails?" I'd want to just riff with him, see if we can spark something together. After that initial brainstorm, I'd probably pull back, gather my own thoughts and his contributions, and then start trying to shape them into something more concrete for discussion with others like Yoshida or Yamada, before finally presenting something coherent to Yamaguchi.
